does: This paper introduces RedactionBench, a manually annotated benchmark dataset for evaluating personally identifiable information (PII) redaction across 200 diverse documents spanning 11 domains, mostly derived from real-world sources. The work distinguishes redaction from standard entity recognition by grounding it in contextual integrity theory, recognizing that whether information requires removal depends on context, holder, purpose, and domain rather than just its type. The authors also propose R-Score, a character-level evaluation metric that treats semantically equivalent redactions uniformly regardless of masking style variations and focuses on meaningful privacy violations rather than superficial formatting differences. Their evaluation across Named Entity Recognition models, entity extraction Small Language Models, and frontier LLMs with agentic tools reveals that contextual redaction remains an unsolved problem, with substantial performance gaps between different approaches.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research in PII detection and information extraction, particularly Named Entity Recognition systems that identify sensitive data types in text. However, it advances beyond existing benchmarks like those in standard NLP evaluation that treat redaction as a pure entity extraction task. The paper directly responds to limitations in prior work that conflate mechanical extraction with privacy-aware decision-making, drawing on contextual integrity frameworks from privacy research that emphasize context-dependent sensitivity. It combines insights from privacy literature recognizing that sensitivity varies by domain, holder, and purpose with practical NLP evaluation needs. The work also engages with recent scaling trends in LLMs applied to sensitive domains, where off-the-shelf models lack domain-specific privacy reasoning despite increasing deployment in healthcare, legal, and financial applications.
enables: This benchmark and metric framework enable more rigorous evaluation of privacy-preserving NLP systems and support development of context-aware redaction models that go beyond entity type classification. The work points toward future systems that incorporate domain knowledge, institutional context, and privacy semantics into redaction decisions rather than relying on shallow pattern matching. RedactionBench provides infrastructure for training and evaluating domain-specialized redaction agents, potentially informing agentic systems designed for sensitive document processing. The contextual integrity grounding suggests follow-up research on explainable redaction decisions, multi-stakeholder privacy policies, and privacy-aware fine-tuning of language models. It also opens directions for developing lightweight, domain-specific redaction tools alongside evaluation of frontier models, supporting safer deployment of LLMs in regulated industries like healthcare and law.
